summary: The author presents a preliminary solution that replaces traditional multiple sequence alignment (MSA) with a large RNA language model to capture evolutionary information. This is combined with a smaller downstream structure prediction model, initially leveraging five out of one hundred available structure models. The writeup outlines future experimental directions, including energy-based scoring for model selection, clustering, converting (N,C,P) frames and distance maps to a C1 backbone, and applying quantization/fp16 to extend sequence length to 800.
extensive_summary:
## Approach & Architecture
The baseline replaces standard MSA with a large RNA language model to model evolutionary information. This is paired with a smaller downstream structure prediction model. For the current leaderboard score, the author uses five out of 100 available smaller structure models, explicitly noting that incorporating more structure models improves performance.

## Experimental Plans
Future iterations aim to implement energy scoring to select the best model outputs, potentially followed by clustering. The author also plans to train a model to convert (N,C,P) frames and distance maps into a C1 backbone. To handle longer sequences, quantization and fp16 precision are planned to extend the model length to 800.

## Hardware & Resources
Experiments were run on an HP Z8 Fury-G5 Data Science Workstation equipped with 2x Nvidia Ada A6000 GPUs (48 GB VRAM each), an Intel Xeon w7-3455 CPU (24 cores/48 threads), and 256 GB RAM.
competition_flow: Sequence data is processed through an RNA language model for evolutionary features, passed to a downstream structure prediction model, and will be refined via energy scoring and clustering before submission.
insights: Using an RNA language model instead of MSA effectively captures evolutionary information.
Incorporating more available structure models consistently improves the leaderboard score.
